transcript.text: You're going to join us too, correct?
Sorry?
Was Marcel going to join as well?
He is literally just coming in right now.
just got away.
Sorry, I was just finishing up an interview.
How's it going?
Going just fine.
We're building quickly and we're getting to the stage where we're potentially coming out of beta in a few weeks here.
And so I thought it'd be a good time to start thinking about how do we let more people know than just the small kind of travel community and AI community that we exist.
Yeah.
And so through Madrona and through a couple other things, I found you guys and thought I'd learn a little bit more about GrowthX and what you guys are doing.
Happy to share what we're doing as well.
And then we can kind of go from there to see if it makes sense.
Yeah, that's perfect.
Maybe we'd love to, Kyle told me a little bit about, like, background influence.
Transparency didn't get a chance to go like crazy in details, you know, but we do have, we've got half an hour, right?
Yeah, yeah, we've got half an hour.
We'd love to learn a little bit more about like the, what, like the unique approach that y'all are taking.
kind of understand conceptually and I have some context around like, for instance, we're working with engine.com.
They used to be called Hotel Engine.

So like, I think, I don't know how much overlap there is in the audience, but at least like I know conceptually and then some of the directors in our team were at like TripAdvisor and I know based on your background too, like, so, so I have enough context to be dangerous, but probably not enough to, to pretend like I know your journey.
So I'd love to hear a little bit more.

I'll start by giving the massive disclaimer that travel seems really, really simple and seems like one category, but probably is about 200 different categories and is actually insanely complicated, mainly because without giving a massive travel technique.
Technology history lesson, travel was actually one of the first technology and networked computer type of industries back in the 60s and 70s.
It was one of the first reasons why modems were really created for travel agents to be able to log into these things called GDSs.
You know, the IBM's initial computers, their kind of supercomputers were sold to Amadeus and to Sabre to power these reservation engines.
There's still a massive data center in Germany that every time you check into a flight, if you're in Europe, it actually validates it against the servers that are sitting in there.
You know, to really kind of go back on the world, you know how every airline has a two-letter code?
Like UA for United, AS for Alaska Airlines, DL for Delta.
Well, most memory locations are 8-bit memory locations, right?
And so the six...
Character PNR that you see, which is your flight confirmation code, is actually the two letters of the airline code plus the six of the PNR.
That's the memory location for your, at least that was originally designed, for your passenger record.
That's kind of how old you feel right now, you know.
That's exactly what all this junk was.
That was an IBM back in the day, you know.
There you go.
There you go.
You can look up Amadeus and IBM's history and you'll learn and probably have flashbacks.
Anyway, so long story short, I started my career as a developer, as an engineer.
I'd say fairly quickly, five years in, kind of moved out of hardcore coding and more into talking to people and explaining what software does.
Today, you would call that a solutions engineer or solutions architect.
Back then, I was the tech guy who would go join the salespeople to explain what the hell the software did.
Did a couple of startups early in my career, fast forward a bunch, and I joined Expedia in largely analytics and product management roles.
I was there for about 15 years and ended up, I'd say my kind of last two or three senior roles was chief product officer for the consumer division for Expedia Group.
So that was over hotels.com, Expedia, Orbitz, all of the things.
Everything you guys think of as any of the Expedia properties was my product and strategy group.
There was a, you know, the reason I say consumer is there's a fairly large API and partner business, which I had a counterpart who had a product for.
I was also the overall GM for the transportation business, air, car, cruise, things like that.
But most importantly for Otto, which is the company I'm now the CEO and founder of, I was head of marketing and chief product officer for Agencia, which was the corporate travel.
Arm of Expedia that was sold slash merged with Amex GBT, American Express Global Business Travel.
And so now Agencia, you know, the software is part of Amex GBT.
And that's really where I learned kind of the nuances and the weirdness and the strangeness of corporate travel or business travel.

And so if you guys have worked for large companies, certainly, you know, Marcel, IBM, you would have been given, you know, depending on your years back, you probably would have been given a 1-800 number to call and book any business travel.
And then later on, you know, a website that worked or maybe didn't.
I remember that.
That probably looked more akin to like Microsoft Word a la 2005 than it did any corporate website you'd know today.
And that's largely where the corporate travel technology, internet industry.
Stayed.
Concur came along and, you know, is really a front-end booking tool that sits on top of a bunch of different travel agencies.

So most of the large six travel agencies all use the same software on top of it to the point where a lot of employees who work for companies don't even know that they're using Amex, GBT, don't even know they're using direct travel or agency or any of those.
They think they're using Concur just because that's kind of the front-end.
And so I left Expedia, you know, kind of, I wouldn't say bummed around, but kind of, you know, tried to figure out what I wanted to do next.
I was just burned out from Expedia and was at that stage where I'm like, am I going to go consult with companies?
Am I going to go sit on some boards or am I going to do my own thing?
And so I decided to jump in with this, you know, travel agent based on AI technology.
I'm not really calling it a travel agent AI.
You know, I know that gets funding and whatnot.
But that's not the goal.
The goal is really to create an executive assistant that knows your travel preferences, handles everything when something goes wrong, which happens a lot, and just really takes care of things.
So you can say, hey, I need to head down to our San Francisco office for a meeting on Wednesday, get me in Tuesday night and home on Thursday, book me the same hotel as last time.
And Otto will just do that.
We have flights and hotels.
We don't just say, here's some flights, here's some hotels, go figure it out yourself.
We have full API connections into, you know, two, three hundred plus flights, over six million hotels and properties that we can book travelers into.
Full cancellation, selecting your seats, loyalty numbers.
If you can get free seats because you're MVP on Masked Airlines or Medallion on Delta, it'll select that for you.
If you want to be so complicated and so weirdly nuanced as, you know, when I'm flying east, I want to sit on the right-hand side of the plane on the window.
When I'm flying west, I want to sit on the left-hand side of the plane by the window.
Otherwise, if I can't get one of those, you know, put me as far forward in an aisle as possible, unless it's a Boeing 737 MAX, in which case I'll take a window seat.
can figure all that out.
flying with two dogs and my two-year-old, in which case get me the  out of here.
It's going to be a nightmare no matter what.
Exactly.
So think of it as just hyper-personalization of travel.
The best I would say is it's more akin to an executive assistant for travel, like so that EA that really gets to know you, as opposed to a booking engine.
This is not where you're going to go on and say, hey, what's the cheapest flight to somewhere in the Caribbean in the month of October for my wife and two kids, right?
It's, it's, it's, this is not like.
Go find the most Instagram-worthy beach locations and build me an itinerary, dreaming type of things.
This is a very transactional, actionable type of a product.
So that's the highlights of it.
We've spent about a year and a half now in building mode.
We've had probably over 1,000 beta users who have tested out the product and given us ton of feedback.
It's getting better every day.
But at some time, we actually have to go out there and start getting these, you know, semi-unmanaged business travelers.
And that's a fancy travel term for basically saying somebody who is not using one of these corporate travel sites, you know, potentially GrowthX.
I don't know if you guys just kind of book your own travel.
But at some point, your CFO, your CEO, someone's going to look at that and go, hold on a sec.
We probably should have some sort of travel policy.
We should probably have some management of it.
I should probably know where all my employees are.
And then they can start using something like an auto or even you guys as employees can start using auto.
It doesn't cost anything.
We make money on the backside from commissions.
But essentially, it's just a better experience for users than rolling their own and going to Alaska or Delta directly and then going to Marriott directly.
The benefits of it all being in one place is you can say, hey, I need to move my trip back to the next week.
And then auto will just move your flight, move your hotel, and all the different pieces of it as well.
Yeah, that's great.
And so what I'm hearing is your buyer right now is a fair to assume that is more of a business traveler that travels often as particular about their needs.
Yeah, Road Warrior is kind of the marketing terminology for it or whatever the terminology is.
And Road Warrior, different companies define it as different.
But it's really someone who takes six or more trips a year for work.
And so I would say that's one type of a customer.

I would imagine that in about a year, you know, it's also going to be that CFO or head of finance or CEO or maybe even kind of the, you know, the, I'm sure they're called something different, but the equivalent of an office manager, there's somebody who's kind of taking care of all the employees in a location so that they can start helping out their employees and onboarding and things like that.
But for now, it's really targeting those road warriors who are just looking for a better experience.
then last one, and then I would love to jump in and give you some like high level as well.
But if you have kind of this magic one, right, like six months from now, what are you optimizing for that you think like we might be able to help?
And obviously, I'll show you a bunch of stuff and be super transparent about it.
But always.
Just really curious of like, what is really important for you in the business in the next six to 12 months, you know?
So, I mean, the main thing, quite honestly, right now is just getting more transactions.
The more volume, the more searches we have, the more we see and the more auto can see what users are actually selecting, what they're actually booking.
The better the model can get, the more we can refine it, the more we, you know, it can start selecting the right things.
And so, it's not necessarily volume for the sake of building out, you know, ARR or things like that.
I mean, yes, that matters.
But really, for the next six to nine months, if you get that timeframe, it's just more transactional volume going through.
Because then the system can get better.
Yeah, that makes sense.
And so, why not just dump a bunch of money in ads?
We've done some of that.
Um, you know, it's, we're getting users over who are doing ads.
You've got a lot of people who are just kind of exploring, think it's a cool concept, um, and are not necessarily doing the whole lot yet.

Um, so I think in some ways we're, we're targeting, we're, we're doing a lot of kind of the, um, the road warrior type of small form videos, you know, the, the 20 to 30 second videos that are on loop on, on Instagram.
Um, and, and we're getting a decent CPL cost per lead as far as, you know, getting people to sign up for auto and go in.
Uh, we're not really seeing the same repeat rates.
Um, and I think it's pretty broad.
Um, you know, we're not necessarily going targeting consultants.
We're not necessarily going and targeting, you know, people working for law firms or, or bankers and things like that, who, who are the ones who are going to be traveling a whole lot.
Yeah.
Sales, sales, sales reps, et cetera.
Yeah, that, that makes sense.
I think like, so, um, I can skip.

A lot of the background stuff, unless you think it would be super helpful to know more of the background, but happy to kind of do a little bit of that or just jump into and kind of show you a little bit of kind of how some of the companies we work with, how we do it, like what we actually do.
Let's jump into it.
Just jump right in.

So a little bit of just the TLDR here is we start a process.
So one of the things we learned was most companies don't have really good inputs.
It's not about I need to automate and 1,000x this, and I know exactly what works, right?

And so they don't have opinions.
And so a lot of what we do is we go through this what we call strategy spread.
What we're trying to do is figure out how get the right inputs from you and from your space and learn as deeply as

So we'll go, we'll have a kickoff session, we'll detail everything we heard from that, then we'll start to build out, based on all the docs you send us and everything else, what we call artifacts, right?

So artifacts are, there's this concept now of like context engineering that's coming up more, but think of artifacts as like the distilling down a lot of noise into really high quality signals that can be pulled dynamically into really complex workflows that are constantly being refreshed, but that allows us to, you know, inform and give the right, like, here's the big picture of everything we're doing with AI and humans, right?

And so it's both for our editors and the people that are working with you to know like what your business is and what it does and all the context, but it's also perfect for us to make sure we're doing deep research on a topic, we get into the right context and it has the right relevance, right?
And so we're doing everything from like, not only understanding your customers, like, you know, ICP, all of that, the different jobs.
To be done, you know, and kind of going beyond that.
And then I get like all the right inputs as well, right?
Okay, this is, this makes sense.
This doesn't make sense.
And as we continue to learn more about your business, and I'll show you here shortly, like where this gets all fatted to, right?

And we start to say like, hey, let's start to identify clusters of our topics, right?
Like what are the areas, the topic areas that we really want to own and win?
And not just like overall, like, but more specific to your company, to what you want to do and what mindshare you want to own in your audience's, you know, mind and kind of what they consume, right?
And then really just breaking it down for how you see the world, right?
Like in this case, engine.com, I wanted to use them as an example because it's...
I know them fairly well.
Yeah.

Within these clusters, within these industries, within these personas, right?
And then, like, what we're doing then is going and saying, hey, let's go identify.
In this case, they have a lot of content, but we're identifying both internal and external opportunities for them.
External are the ones that their competitors are already ranking for.
Internals, they have a ton of pages.
So in your case, probably more relevant than you, obviously, right?
And then we start to go through this process where we're identifying hundreds of opportunities and then starting to execute those opportunities, right?
And so that's the other side of this.

Like, we can't scale okay, right?

Where do we get these unique inputs?
Like, what's the right angle?
How do we position this?
And how do we create value?

And so in this case, for example, like we're going and saying, hey, here's like a calibration guide and kind how we're thinking about this based on everything we heard from you.

Here's how we would have written that same sentence, right?
Or the same paragraph.
And then that enables us to just go through the calibration.

So by the time you're done with this, it might have gone through, you know, literally like hundreds of steps, right?

Obviously, like Ramp is just an example of, like, at a larger scale, you know, seeing some of the results we're driving, and I can give you a ton of other examples, you know.
So, and then there's a few others, like exec.com that I was just showing you.

And we're working directly with the founder CEO, you know, and they don't have massive, large teams.

can become customers.

things?

really like, hey, we can't launch this supplement brand, and somebody comes on our website, and we have zero content for women around the topics that we're saying we're experts in.

creating content for one single person.

Yes, we're fairly unique in that, like, I try to set this up in a way that, like, I would have wanted to buy it as a CMO, right?
And so essentially what we do is we do an eight-week strategy sprint, and that de-risks everything because, like, there's no long-term commitment on that eight weeks.
in that eight weeks, we're doing, like, all these artifacts.
We're doing our entire content strategy.

## Meeting Purpose

[Explore potential collaboration between GrowthX and Otto for content strategy and organic growth](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Otto is an AI-powered travel assistant targeting frequent business travelers ("road warriors")](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=407.0)
  - [GrowthX offers comprehensive content strategy and execution services, from initial strategy to daily content publishing](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=858.0)
  - [Otto's primary goal is increasing transaction volume to improve their AI model and user experience](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=725.0)
  - [GrowthX's process involves an 8-week strategy sprint ($20k) followed by ongoing execution ($9k-$15k/month)](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1637.0)

## Topics

### Otto's Business Model and Target Audience

  - [AI-powered travel assistant for frequent business travelers](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=415.0)
  - [Focuses on personalized, executive assistant-like experience for travel booking and management](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=422.0)
  - [Target: "Road Warriors" (6+ trips/year) and potentially CFOs/office managers in the future](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=657.0)
  - [Currently in beta with \~1,000 users, preparing for wider launch](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=560.0)

### Otto's Current Challenges and Goals

  - [Increasing transaction volume to improve AI model and user experience](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=730.0)
  - [Targeting the right audience segments (e.g., consultants, lawyers, sales reps)](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=657.0)
  - [Building authority and visibility in a competitive travel tech landscape](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=566.0)
  - [Limited internal resources for content creation and marketing (Michael as solo non-tech team member)](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1800.0)

### GrowthX's Content Strategy Approach

  - [Comprehensive process from initial strategy to daily content publishing](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=858.0)
  - [Creation of "artifacts" - distilled, high-quality signals for consistent context across content](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=912.0)
  - [Custom workflow engine combining AI and human editing for efficient content production](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1180.0)
  - [Focus on creating unique, valuable content tailored to specific audience segments](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1729.0)

### GrowthX's Service Model and Pricing

  - [8-week strategy sprint ($20,000) for initial planning and artifact creation](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1637.0)
  - [Ongoing execution phase ($9,000-$15,000/month) for content production and management](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1681.0)
  - [Full-service approach, including content strategy, creation, design, and publishing](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1713.0)
  - [Aimed at replacing an entire in-house content team](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1713.0)

### Potential Collaboration Timeline

  - [6-8 weeks until potential project start (aligning with Otto's public launch plans)](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1929.0)
  - [8-week strategy sprint followed by ongoing execution](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1942.0)
  - [Emphasis on starting content publication as early as possible, even before full GrowthX engagement](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1980.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Schedule follow-up meeting later in the week or early next week](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=2040.0)
  - [Michael to consider GrowthX's offering in relation to Otto's launch timeline and goals](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1926.0)
  - [Explore potential for starting content creation and publishing ahead of full GrowthX engagement](https://fathom.video/share/yv1viCLr28r4Nx_xdUTU1gCY9fVkVNTw?tab=summary&timestamp=1988.0)
